a: Matthew opens with Jesus' genealogy from Abraham through David, the virgin birth, the visit of the magi, the flight to Egypt, and Herod's slaughter of the infants (1–2). John the Baptist prepares the way and baptizes Jesus, who is then led into the wilderness and tempted three times by Satan (3–4). Jesus begins his Galilean ministry, calls his first disciples (Peter, Andrew, James, John), and preaches "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand."
summary: Jesus' genealogy and birth, the magi, the baptism by John, the wilderness temptation, and the start of Galilean ministry.

a: The Sermon on the Mount, Jesus' great manifesto of the kingdom. It opens with the Beatitudes and the call to be salt and light (5), reinterprets the law's true intent ("you have heard… but I say"), and teaches on prayer (the Lord's Prayer), treasure, and anxiety (6), closing with the narrow gate and the wise and foolish builders (7).
summary: The Sermon on the Mount: the Beatitudes, the law's true intent, the Lord's Prayer, and the wise and foolish builders.

a: The heart of the Galilean ministry, alternating mighty works and teaching. Jesus heals the leper, the centurion's servant, and stills the storm (8–9), commissions the Twelve (10), responds to John the Baptist and the Pharisees (11–12), and teaches the kingdom parables: sower, weeds, mustard seed, pearl (13). He feeds the five thousand and four thousand, walks on water, and Peter confesses him as the Christ at Caesarea Philippi (14–16). The transfiguration, the first passion predictions, and teaching on humility, forgiveness, and church discipline follow (17–18).
summary: Galilean ministry: healings, the sending of the Twelve, the kingdom parables, Peter's confession, and the transfiguration.

a: Jesus journeys toward Jerusalem, teaching on divorce, riches (the rich young ruler), and servant greatness (19–20). He enters Jerusalem triumphantly, cleanses the temple, and confronts the religious leaders through parables and controversy questions, climaxing in the woes against the scribes and Pharisees (21–23). The Olivet Discourse then predicts the temple's destruction and Christ's return, with the parables of the ten virgins, the talents, and the sheep and the goats (24–25).
summary: The journey to Jerusalem, the triumphal entry, conflict with the leaders, and the Olivet Discourse on the end.

a: The passion and resurrection. Jesus is anointed at Bethany, shares the Last Supper instituting the Lord's Supper, prays in Gethsemane, and is betrayed by Judas and arrested (26). He is tried before Caiaphas and Pilate, mocked, crucified, and buried, with Peter denying him three times (26–27). On the third day he rises; the women find the empty tomb, and the risen Christ gives the Great Commission in Galilee: make disciples of all nations.
summary: The Last Supper, Gethsemane, the trials, the crucifixion, the resurrection, and the Great Commission.

a: Mark opens abruptly with "the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God." John the Baptist prepares the way and baptizes Jesus, the Spirit descends, and the Father's voice affirms him; Jesus is then tempted in the wilderness. He begins preaching the kingdom, calls Peter, Andrew, James, and John, and launches a busy day of ministry in Capernaum—casting out a demon, healing Peter's mother-in-law, and cleansing a leper. Mark's hallmark "immediately" drives the fast pace.
summary: The beginnings: John's preparation, Jesus' baptism and temptation, and the call of the first disciples in Galilee.

a: Jesus' Galilean ministry amid mounting conflict. He forgives and heals the paralytic, calls Levi, and disputes over fasting and the Sabbath (2–3). He teaches in parables (sower, mustard seed), stills the storm, casts out Legion, and heals Jairus' daughter and the bleeding woman (4–5). He is rejected at Nazareth, sends out the Twelve, and after John the Baptist's death feeds the five thousand and walks on water (6), then debates the Pharisees over tradition and defilement (7).
summary: Galilean ministry and rising conflict: healings, the parables, the storm stilled, the feeding of the five thousand, and disputes over tradition.

a: The pivotal central section, "the way" to Jerusalem. Jesus feeds the four thousand, then Peter confesses him as the Christ at Caesarea Philippi, and Jesus three times predicts his suffering, death, and resurrection (8–10). The transfiguration reveals his glory, but the disciples repeatedly fail to grasp the cross, arguing over greatness. Jesus teaches discipleship as cross-bearing, welcomes children, meets the rich young ruler, and frames his mission: the Son of Man came to serve and "give his life as a ransom for many."
summary: Peter's confession, the three passion predictions, the transfiguration, and the call to cross-bearing discipleship.

a: Jesus enters Jerusalem to shouts of "Hosanna," curses the fruitless fig tree, and cleanses the temple (11). He silences the leaders with parables and controversy questions—the tenants, taxes to Caesar, the resurrection, the greatest commandment—and warns against the scribes while commending the widow's offering (12). The Olivet Discourse then foretells the temple's destruction, coming tribulation, the Son of Man's return, and the call to watchfulness (13).
summary: The triumphal entry, the temple cleansing, controversies with the leaders, and the Olivet Discourse.

a: The passion and resurrection. Jesus is anointed at Bethany, shares the Last Supper, prays in Gethsemane, and is betrayed by Judas and arrested (14). He is tried before the Sanhedrin and Pilate as Peter denies him; he is mocked, crucified, cries out, dies as the centurion confesses him Son of God, and is buried (15). On the first day the women find the tomb empty and a young man announces, "He has risen" (16).
summary: The anointing, Last Supper, Gethsemane, trials, crucifixion, burial, and the empty tomb.

a: Luke's infancy narrative, addressed to Theophilus as an orderly account. The angel Gabriel announces the births of John the Baptist to Zechariah and Jesus to the virgin Mary; Mary visits Elizabeth and sings the Magnificat (1). Jesus is born in Bethlehem, announced to shepherds, circumcised, and presented in the temple where Simeon and Anna recognize him; the chapter closes with the twelve-year-old Jesus in the temple (2).
summary: The annunciations and births of John and Jesus, the Magnificat, the shepherds, and the boy Jesus in the temple.

a: The preparation for ministry. John the Baptist preaches repentance and baptizes Jesus, followed by Jesus' genealogy traced back to Adam (3). Jesus is tempted forty days in the wilderness, then begins his ministry; at the Nazareth synagogue he reads Isaiah 61 and declares it fulfilled, provoking rejection (4). He moves to Capernaum, casts out an unclean spirit, heals Peter's mother-in-law, and preaches the kingdom throughout Galilee.
summary: John's baptism, Jesus' genealogy and temptation, and the Nazareth synagogue declaration that begins his ministry.

a: The Galilean ministry. Jesus calls Peter after the great catch, heals the leper and paralytic, calls Levi, and chooses the Twelve (5–6). He delivers the Sermon on the Plain, raises the widow of Nain's son, forgives the sinful woman, and stills the storm (6–8). He casts out Legion, raises Jairus' daughter, feeds the five thousand, and receives Peter's confession; the transfiguration and first passion prediction follow (9).
summary: Galilean ministry: the call of disciples, the Sermon on the Plain, healings, Peter's confession, and the transfiguration.

a: Luke's distinctive travel narrative, as Jesus "set his face to go to Jerusalem" (9:51). He sends out the seventy-two and teaches through parables unique to Luke—the good Samaritan, the rich fool, the lost sheep, coin, and prodigal son, and the rich man and Lazarus (10–16). He teaches on prayer, the kingdom, humility, riches, and discipleship, heals ten lepers, blesses children, meets the rich ruler, and converts Zacchaeus at Jericho (17–19).
summary: The travel narrative to Jerusalem with Luke's parables: the good Samaritan, prodigal son, and Zacchaeus.

a: In the temple Jesus disputes with the leaders, tells the parable of the tenants, answers on taxes and the resurrection, and gives the Olivet predictions, commending the widow's gift (20–21). He shares the Last Supper, prays on the Mount of Olives, and is betrayed, arrested, tried before the Sanhedrin, Pilate, and Herod, and crucified (22–23). He rises on the third day, appears on the road to Emmaus and to the disciples, and ascends after promising the Spirit (24).
summary: Temple teaching and the Olivet predictions, the passion, the resurrection, the Emmaus road, and the ascension.

a: John's prologue declares the eternal Word who was with God and was God, became flesh, and dwelt among us full of grace and truth—the light shining in the darkness. John the Baptist witnesses to him and points out Jesus as "the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world." Jesus then calls his first disciples—Andrew, Peter, Philip, and Nathanael—who confess him as the Son of God and King of Israel.
summary: The prologue of the Word made flesh, John the Baptist's witness, and the call of the first disciples.

a: The Book of Signs, structured around seven miracles revealing Jesus' glory. He turns water to wine, cleanses the temple, and meets Nicodemus and the Samaritan woman (2–4). He heals at Bethesda, feeds the five thousand, declares himself the bread of life, and the light of the world (5–9), giving sight to the man born blind. He teaches as the good shepherd, raises Lazarus from the dead, and enters Jerusalem as the crowds turn against him (10–12).
summary: The Book of Signs: water to wine, Nicodemus, the bread of life, the good shepherd, and the raising of Lazarus.

a: The Upper Room Discourse on the night before the cross. Jesus washes the disciples' feet, foretells his betrayal, and gives the new commandment to love one another (13). He comforts them as the way, the truth, and the life, promises the Holy Spirit as Helper, and teaches the vine and the branches (14–16). He concludes with the High Priestly Prayer, interceding for himself, his disciples, and all who would believe, that they may be one (17).
summary: The Upper Room Discourse: the foot washing, the promise of the Spirit, the vine, and the High Priestly Prayer.

a: The passion, resurrection, and epilogue. Jesus is betrayed in the garden, arrested, and tried before Annas, Caiaphas, and Pilate as Peter denies him; he is crucified, declares "It is finished," and is buried (18–19). On the first day Mary Magdalene finds the empty tomb, and the risen Jesus appears to the disciples and to doubting Thomas (20). In the epilogue by the Sea of Galilee he provides a great catch, restores Peter with "Feed my sheep," and commissions his followers (21).
summary: The passion, the crucifixion, the resurrection appearances to Thomas, and the restoration of Peter.

a: Luke's second volume opens with the risen Jesus' forty days of instruction, his promise of the Spirit, and his ascension, followed by the choice of Matthias to replace Judas (1). At Pentecost the Holy Spirit descends with wind and tongues of fire; the disciples speak in other languages, and Peter preaches Christ's death and resurrection from Joel and the Psalms. About three thousand are baptized, and the early church devotes itself to teaching, fellowship, breaking of bread, and prayer (2).
summary: The ascension, the choice of Matthias, the coming of the Spirit at Pentecost, and Peter's first sermon.

a: The church's witness in Jerusalem amid growing opposition. Peter heals the lame man at the temple and preaches, leading to arrest before the Sanhedrin (3–4). The believers share possessions; Ananias and Sapphira are struck dead for lying to the Spirit, and the apostles are arrested and freed by an angel (5). Seven deacons including Stephen are chosen (6); Stephen's sweeping speech indicts the leaders, and he is stoned as the first martyr while Saul approves (7).
summary: Jerusalem witness: Peter's temple healing, Ananias and Sapphira, the seven deacons, and Stephen's martyrdom.

a: The gospel spreads beyond Jerusalem. Persecution scatters believers; Philip evangelizes Samaria and the Ethiopian eunuch (8). Saul is converted on the Damascus road and begins to preach (9). Peter is led to Cornelius, and the Spirit falls on the Gentiles, confirming that God shows no partiality (10–11). The church at Antioch grows and first bears the name "Christians," while Herod kills James and is struck down after Peter's miraculous prison escape (12).
summary: The gospel beyond Jerusalem: Philip in Samaria, Saul's conversion, Cornelius, and the church at Antioch.

a: Paul's missionary journeys. From Antioch, Paul and Barnabas are sent on the first journey through Cyprus and Asia Minor (13–14). The Jerusalem Council affirms Gentiles are saved by grace apart from circumcision (15). On the second journey Paul, with Silas, crosses into Macedonia, founds churches at Philippi, Thessalonica, Berea, and Corinth, and preaches at the Areopagus in Athens (16–18). The third journey centers on Ephesus for over two years, with the silversmiths' riot, before Paul heads back toward Jerusalem (19–20).
summary: Paul's missionary journeys, the Jerusalem Council, and ministry in Philippi, Athens, Corinth, and Ephesus.

a: Paul's arrest and journey to Rome. He is seized in the Jerusalem temple, addresses the mob, and is rescued by Roman soldiers (21–22). He defends himself before the Sanhedrin, escapes a plot, and is sent to Caesarea, where he is tried before Felix and Festus and appeals to Caesar (23–25). After testifying before King Agrippa (26), he sails for Rome, survives shipwreck on Malta (27), and arrives to preach the kingdom of God unhindered while under house arrest (28).
summary: Paul's arrest in Jerusalem, his defenses and appeal to Caesar, the shipwreck, and arrival in Rome.
